CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:70

General Instructions :

  1. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Section A : Questions number 1 to 5 are very short answer questions and carry 1 mark each.
  3. Section B : Questions number 6 to 12 are short answer questions and carry 2 marks each.
  4. Section C : Questions number 13 to 24 are also short answer questions and carry 3 marks each.
  5. Section D : Questions number 25 to 27 are long answer questions and carry 5 marks each.
  6. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in two questions of one mark, two questions of two marks, four questions of three marks and all the three questions of five marks weightage. You have to attempt only one of the choices in such questions.
  7. Use of log tables, if necessary. Use of calculators is not allowed.

SECTION A
1.

Write the IUPAC name of the following compound:

Solution:


IUPAC name of the given compound is But-3-en-2-one.

 1
2.

Arrange the following in increasing order of their acidic character:
Benzoic acid, Phenol, Cresol

Solution:

Increasing order of acidic character is:
Cresol < Phenol < Benzoic Acid

 1
3.

Write IUPAC name of the complex [Co(en)2 (H2O) (CN)]2+

Solution:

IUPAC name of the complex [Co(en)2 (H2O) (CN)]2+ is Aqua cyano bis ethylenediamine Cobalt(III) ion.

 1

OR

Using IUPAC norms, write the formula of Ammonium tetrafluoridocobaltate(II).

Solution:

Formula of Ammonium tetra fluoro cobaltate(II) is (NH4)2 [CoF4].

 1
4.

What is the difference between a glycosidic linkage and a peptide linkage?

Solution:

Glycosidic linkage: It is the linkage which joins two monosaccharides through oxygen atom. It is present in carbohydrates.
Peptide Linkage: It is the linkage which joins two amino acids through - CO-NH- bond. It is present in proteins.

 1

OR

What is the difference between Nucleotide and Nucleoside?

Solution:
Nucleoside Nucleotides
1. A nucleoside consists of a nitrogenous base covalently attached to a sugar (ribose or deoxyribose) but without the phosphate group. 1. A nucleotide consists of a nitrogenous base, a sugar (ribose or deoxyribose) and one to three phosphate groups.
2. Several nucleoside analogues are used as antiviral or anticancer agents. 2. Malfunctioning nucleotides are one of the main causes of all cancers known of today.
3. Nucleoside = Sugar + Base 3. Nucleotide = Sugar + Base + Phosphate
4.Examples of nucleosides are cytidine, uridine. 4.Examples of nucleotides are adenosine, guanosine.
1
5.

Why is chloroform kept in dark coloured bottles?

Solution:

Chloroform gets oxidised slowly by air in the presence of light to form an extremely poisonous gas phosgene (carbonyl chloride). Therefore to avoid any exposure to air and sunlight, it is kept in dark coloured bottles completely filled so that air is kept out.
{tex}2CHC{l_3}{\text{ + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\xrightarrow[{light}]{}{\text{2COC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + 2HCl }}{/tex}

 1

SECTION B
6.

What happens when AgCl is doped with CdCl2? What is the name of this defect?

Solution:

 A CdCl2 doping leads to cation-vacancy defect in the crystal. Two Ag+ ions are replaced by one Cd2+ ion. The Cd2+ ion occupies the site of one of the Ag+ ion and a cation vacancy is created at the site of the other Ag+ ion. This defect is also called impurity defect.
 

 2

OR

What type of defect is shown by NaCl in

  1. stoichiometric defects, and
  2. non-stoichiometric defects?
Solution:
  1. In stoichiometric defects, Schottky defect is shown by NaCl.
  2. In non-stoichiometric defects, Metal excess defect (due to anionic vacancies) is shown by NaCl.
 2
7.
  1. Out of 0.1 molal aqueous solution of glucose and 0.1 molal aqueous solution of KCl, which one will have higher boiling point and why?
  2. Predict whether van’t Hoff factor, (i) is less than one or greater than one in the following:
    1. CH3COOH dissolved in water
    2. CH3COOH dissolved in benzene
Solution:
  1. 0.1 molal KCl will have higher boiling point because KCl undergoes dissociation whereas glucose does not undergo dissociation.
    1. Acetic acid dissociates in water. Therefore, Van’t Hoff factor(i) > 1
    2. Acetic acid associates in benzene. Therefore, Van’t Hoff factor(i) < 1
 2
8.

Complete and balance the following equations :

  1. C + H2SO4 (conc.) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}
  2. XeF2 + PF5 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}
Solution:
  1. {tex}C + 2H_2SO_4 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2SO_2 + 2H_2O{/tex}
  2. {tex}XeF_2 + PF_5 \rightarrow [XeF]^+ [PF_6]^-{/tex}
 2

OR

Write balanced chemical equations involved in the following reactions:

  1. Fluorine gas reacts with water.
  2. Phosphine gas is absorbed in copper sulphate solution.
Solution:
  1. Reaction of fluorine gas with water:
    2F2 + 2H2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4HF + O2
  2. Reaction of phosphine gas with copper sulphate solution:
    PH3 + 3CuSO4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu3P2 + 3 H2SO4
 2
9.

Draw structures of the following :

  1. H2S2O7
  2. HCIO3
Solution:

  1. structures of H2S2O7 :

  2. structures of HCIO3 :

 2
10.

Write anode and cathode reactions that occur in dry cell. How does a dry cell differ from a mercury cell?

Solution:

Reactions in dry cell:
At Anode: Zn(s) {tex}\to{/tex} Zn2+ + 2e-
At Cathode: MnO2 + NH4+ + e- {tex}\to{/tex} MnO(OH) + NH3
Dry cell has shorter life than mercury cell. Cell potential in mercury cell remains constant but not in dry cell.

 2
11.

Account for the following:

  1. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is not preferred for preparing aromatic primary amines.
  2. On reaction with benzene sulphonyl chloride, primary amine yields product soluble in alkali whereas secondary amine yields product insoluble in alkali.
Solution:
  1. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is not preferred for preparing aromatic primary amines because aryl halide does not undergo nucleophilic substitution reaction.
  2. On reaction with benzene sulphonyl chloride, primary amine yields product soluble in alkali whereas secondary amine yields product insoluble in alkali because of the absence of acidic hydrogen attached to nitrogen (N-H) in the product of secondary amine.
 2
12.

Write structures of compounds A and B in each of the following reactions:

Solution:
  1. It is sulphonation reaction.
    Sulphonation: Aniline reacts with concentrated sulphuric acid to form anilinium hydrogen sulphate which on heating with sulphuric acid at {tex}453-473 {~K}{/tex} produces p-aminobenzene sulphonic acid, commonly known as sulphanilic acid, as the major product.
  2. According to Hoffman bromamide degradation a primary amine is prepared by treating an amide with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium Hydroxide.

    Primary amines undergo acylation on treating with anhydride by nucleophilic substitution reaction.
 2

SECTION C
13.
  1. Following reaction takes place in the cell:
    Zn(s) + Ag2O(s) + H2O(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+(aq) + 2Ag(s) + 2OH-(aq)
    Calculate {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\mathbf{o}}{/tex} of the reaction.
    [Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\left(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = -0.76 V,
    {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}\right)}^o{/tex} = 0.80 V, 1 F = 96,500 C mol-1]
  2. How can you determine limiting molar conductivity, {tex}\left(\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}\right){/tex} for strong electrolyte and weak electrolyte?
Solution:
  1. {tex}E^{\circ} _ { cell }=E_{(A g+/ A g)}^o-E_{(Zn^{2+}/Zn)}^o{/tex}
    = 0.80 - (-0.76)
    = 1.56V
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go = {tex}-\mathrm{nFE}{ }_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex}
    = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.56
    = -301080 joules/mol
    = -301.080 kJ/mol
